---
title: "Private Repository & Direct Patch Override"
tagline: "Or what to Do When You Need a One-Off Patch"
type: scenario
tool: sbt
---

#### Scenario

*Or what to Do When You Need a One-Off Patch*
This scenario shows a more direct way to use a private, third-party repository. Instead of using a vendor-supplied BOM, we explicitly request the patched version of a dependency in our project's `libraryDependencies`.
This approach is simpler than the BOM method but can be more verbose if many dependencies need to be overridden. It is suitable for one-off patches or when a full BOM is not available.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    subgraph Public Repos
        SpringBoot["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14 (EOL)"]
    end
    subgraph Private Repo
        PatchedStarter["org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter:2.5.14.ACME"]
    end
    Root[Project Root] -- declares --> PatchedStarter

```

#### SBT (build.sbt)
The `build.sbt` adds the private repository to the list of `resolvers` and then directly declares a dependency on the specific patched version.

```{.scala include="/demos/sbt-demo/scenario-17-private-patch/build.sbt" snippet="repo-and-deps"}
```

#### Reader Expectation
Directly declaring a patched version from a private repository ensures the build tool resolves that specific artifact over public alternatives.

#### Why This Happens
Adding a private resolver makes the artifact available, and a direct `libraryDependencies` declaration serves as the primary resolution signal. SBT selects the requested version to fulfill the project requirement.

#### ! Takeaway
A direct version declaration for a dependency is the most straightforward way to ensure a specific version is used, provided that version is available in one of the configured resolvers.

#### ! Related
*   [Scenario 16: Private Repository & BOM Override](../scenario-16-private-bom/guide.qmd) - A more scalable approach using a vendor BOM.
